title: Symmetrical Hair Loss on a Cat's Abdomen: Triage and Common Causes
meta_desc: Understand why your cat has symmetrical belly hair loss, why it is a low-urgency issue, and how a photo helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Symmetrical Hair Loss?

Symmetrical hair loss on a cat's abdomen, also known as feline symmetrical alopecia, is characterized by thinning or bald patches that appear in a matching pattern on both sides of the belly. This condition is most commonly the result of 'overgrooming,' where a cat licks the fur away due to underlying skin irritation, allergies, or psychological stress. It is less a disease in itself and more a symptom of an external trigger or internal discomfort.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Low. While it can be distressing to see your cat losing fur, it is rarely a life-threatening situation. However, it does indicate that your cat is experiencing some level of discomfort or itchiness that needs to be addressed. You should monitor the area for signs of secondary infection, such as extreme redness, oozing, or a foul odor, which would require more prompt attention.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-resolution photo of your cat's abdomen is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photo allows a professional to see the exact pattern of the hair loss and the condition of the skin underneath. It helps differentiate between hair that has been licked off (evidenced by 'stubble' or broken hair shafts) and hair that is falling out from the follicle, which assists the vet in narrowing down whether the cause is behavioral, hormonal, or related to parasites.

faqs.question: Could fleas be the cause even if I don't see any?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many cats have a flea-saliva allergy where a single bite can trigger intense overgrooming and hair loss, even if the cat is strictly indoors.

faqs.question: Is hair loss on the belly a sign of stress?
faqs.answer: It can be. This is called psychogenic alopecia. Cats often overgroom their belly as a soothing mechanism when they feel anxious about changes in their environment.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to diagnose this?
faqs.answer: The vet may perform a skin scraping, a 'flea comb' check, or recommend a food trial to rule out allergies as the primary cause of the overgrooming.
